old_claim.note: Advanced Synthetic Materials provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Postwar expansion from metallurgy into ceramics, polymers, semiconductors, and other engineered materials helped create the need for a unified materials-science discipline.

source_shape.source_locator: MSE History background section describing the 1950s national progression that merged metallurgy with ceramic science, polymer science, and solid-state physics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes materials science education developing in the 1950s by merging metallurgy with ceramics, polymers, and solid-state physics.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That chronology supports synthetic and engineered material classes as part of the predecessor context for the discipline, not as a single operational prerequisite.

ontology_before: Advanced synthetic materials was modeled as a generic enabling dependency for the broad advanced materials science node.
ontology_after: Advanced synthetic materials is modeled as historical field context for the postwar materials-science discipline while chemistry and quantum/solid-state physics remain enabling foundations.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-09-advanced-materials-science-scope.json locks the date and this historical_predecessor edge type.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to computational materials discovery rather than the postwar materials-science discipline.
A stronger history source showed synthetic materials were a required technical input rather than field context.
The graph split materials science and engineering into separate discipline, characterization, and computational-discovery nodes.
short_reason: Synthetic material classes helped motivate the unified discipline, but are better modeled as lineage context than an enabling capability.
